+++ b/.github/workflows/gemini-triage.yml
@@ -0,0 +1,158 @@
+name: '🔀 Gemini Triage'
+
+on:
+  workflow_call:
+    inputs:
+      additional_context:
+        type: 'string'
+        description: 'Any additional context from the request'
+        required: false
+
+concurrency:
+  group: '${{ github.workflow }}-triage-${{ github.event_name }}-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.event.issue.number }}'
+  cancel-in-progress: true
+
+defaults:
+  run:
+    shell: 'bash'
+
+jobs:
+  triage:
+    runs-on: 'ubuntu-latest'
+    timeout-minutes: 7
+    outputs:
+      available_labels: '${{ steps.get_labels.outputs.available_labels }}'
+      selected_labels: '${{ env.SELECTED_LABELS }}'
+    permissions:
+      contents: 'read'
+      id-token: 'write'
+      issues: 'read'
+      pull-requests: 'read'
+    steps:
+      - name: 'Get repository labels'
+        id: 'get_labels'
+        uses: 'actions/github-script@ed597411d8f924073f98dfc5c65a23a2325f34cd' # ratchet:actions/github-script@v8.0.0
+        with:
+          # NOTE: we intentionally do not use the given token. The default
+          # GITHUB_TOKEN provided by the action has enough permissions to read
+          # the labels.
+          script: |-
+            const labels = [];
+            for await (const response of github.paginate.iterator(github.rest.issues.listLabelsForRepo, {
+              owner: context.repo.owner,
+              repo: context.repo.repo,
+              per_page: 100, // Maximum per page to reduce API calls
+            })) {
+              labels.push(...response.data);
+            }
+
+            if (!labels || labels.length === 0) {
+              core.setFailed('There are no issue labels in this repository.')
+            }
+
+            const labelNames = labels.map(label => label.name).sort();
+            core.setOutput('available_labels', labelNames.join(','));
+            core.info(`Found ${labelNames.length} labels: ${labelNames.join(', ')}`);
+            return labelNames;
+
+      - name: 'Run Gemini issue analysis'
+        id: 'gemini_analysis'
+        if: |-
+          ${{ steps.get_labels.outputs.available_labels != '' }}
+        uses: 'google-github-actions/run-gemini-cli@v0' # ratchet:exclude
+        env:
+          GITHUB_TOKEN: '' # Do NOT pass any auth tokens here since this runs on untrusted inputs
+          ISSUE_TITLE: '${{ github.event.issue.title }}'
+          ISSUE_BODY: '${{ github.event.issue.body }}'
+          AVAILABLE_LABELS: '${{ steps.get_labels.outputs.available_labels }}'
+        with:
+          gcp_location: '${{ vars.GOOGLE_CLOUD_LOCATION }}'
+          gcp_project_id: '${{ vars.GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT }}'
+          gcp_service_account: '${{ vars.SERVICE_ACCOUNT_EMAIL }}'
+          gcp_workload_identity_provider: '${{ vars.GCP_WIF_PROVIDER }}'
+          gemini_api_key: '${{ secrets.GEMINI_API_KEY }}'
+          gemini_cli_version: '${{ vars.GEMINI_CLI_VERSION }}'
+          gemini_debug: '${{ fromJSON(vars.GEMINI_DEBUG || vars.ACTIONS_STEP_DEBUG || false) }}'
+          gemini_model: '${{ vars.GEMINI_MODEL }}'
+          google_api_key: '${{ secrets.GOOGLE_API_KEY }}'
+          use_gemini_code_assist: '${{ vars.GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_GCA }}'
+          use_vertex_ai: '${{ vars.GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_VERTEXAI }}'
+          upload_artifacts: '${{ vars.UPLOAD_ARTIFACTS }}'
+          workflow_name: 'gemini-triage'
+          settings: |-
+            {
+              "model": {
+                "maxSessionTurns": 25
+              },
+              "telemetry": {
+                "enabled": true,
+                "target": "local",
+                "outfile": ".gemini/telemetry.log"
+              },
+              "tools": {
+                "core": [
+                  "run_shell_command(echo)"
+                ]
+              }
+            }
+          prompt: '/gemini-triage'
+
+  label:
+    runs-on: 'ubuntu-latest'
+    needs:
+      - 'triage'
+    if: |-
+      ${{ needs.triage.outputs.selected_labels != '' }}
+    permissions:
+      contents: 'read'
+      issues: 'write'
+      pull-requests: 'write'
+    steps:
+      - name: 'Mint identity token'
+        id: 'mint_identity_token'
+        if: |-
+          ${{ vars.APP_ID }}
+        uses: 'actions/create-github-app-token@29824e69f54612133e76f7eaac726eef6c875baf' # ratchet:actions/create-github-app-token@v2
+        with:
+          app-id: '${{ vars.APP_ID }}'
+          private-key: '${{ secrets.APP_PRIVATE_KEY }}'
+          permission-contents: 'read'
+          permission-issues: 'write'
+          permission-pull-requests: 'write'
+
+      - name: 'Apply labels'
+        env:
+          ISSUE_NUMBER: '${{ github.event.issue.number }}'
+          AVAILABLE_LABELS: '${{ needs.triage.outputs.available_labels }}'
+          SELECTED_LABELS: '${{ needs.triage.outputs.selected_labels }}'
+        uses: 'actions/github-script@ed597411d8f924073f98dfc5c65a23a2325f34cd' # ratchet:actions/github-script@v8.0.0
+        with:
+          # Use the provided token so that the "gemini-cli" is the actor in the
+          # log for what changed the labels.
+          github-token: '${{ steps.mint_identity_token.outputs.token || secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN || github.token }}'
+          script: |-
+            // Parse the available labels
+            const availableLabels = (process.env.AVAILABLE_LABELS || '').split(',')
+              .map((label) => label.trim())
+              .sort()
+
+            // Parse the label as a CSV, reject invalid ones - we do this just
+            // in case someone was able to prompt inject malicious labels.
+            const selectedLabels = (process.env.SELECTED_LABELS || '').split(',')
+              .map((label) => label.trim())
+              .filter((label) => availableLabels.includes(label))
+              .sort()
+
+            // Set the labels
+            const issueNumber = process.env.ISSUE_NUMBER;
+            if (selectedLabels && selectedLabels.length > 0) {
+              await github.rest.issues.setLabels({
+                owner: context.repo.owner,
+                repo: context.repo.repo,
+                issue_number: issueNumber,
+                labels: selectedLabels,
+              });
+              core.info(`Successfully set labels: ${selectedLabels.join(',')}`);
+            } else {
+              core.info(`Failed to determine labels to set. There may not be enough information in the issue or pull request.`)
+            }

-1.  **tell me staged changes and unstaged changes** and ask me if I want to stage all the changes
-2.  **Generate a comprehensive commit message** (summarizing the staged changes) 
-3.  **Show the commit message** and ask for confirmation that I want to continue using this commit message

ull Request** with the generated message and a suitable title. Then, **execute the necessary script** to monitor deployment and provide the preview link.
-5.  **Monitor deployment and provide preview link:**
-    *   Continuously check the latest GitHub Actions run on the current branch (`gh run list --branch <branch-name> --limit 1 --json status,url`).      
-    *   Once the run's `status` is "completed" and `conclusion` is "success", get the run ID.
-    *   Retrieve job details for that run (`gh run view <run_id> --json jobs`) to identify the `databaseId` of the "Deploy Preview (PR)" job.
-    *   Fetch the full logs for this job (`gh run view <run_id> --job <job_database_id> --log`).
-    *   Extract the preview URL from the logs. It will be a line similar to `"url": "https://<project-id>--pr-<pr-number>-<random-string>.web.app"`.    
-    *   Present the preview link to the user and ask them to confirm if it works as expected.
-6.  if I say yes, then **merge the branch** so that the production is updated
-7.  **Initiate Release Workflow (Post-Merge):**
-    *   **Determine new version:** Ask me if I want to bump the version as 'patch', 'minor', or 'major'.
-    *   **Update `package.json` & Commit:** Update the `version` field in `package.json` based on my input and commit this change with a message like "R
elease vX.Y.Z".
-    *   **Create Git Tag:** Create an annotated Git tag `vX.Y.Z` on the release commit.
-    *   **Push to `main` & Tags:** Push the `main` branch (with the new version commit) and the new Git tag to the remote.
-8.  **Deploy Production Artifacts:**
-    *   Trigger the CI/CD pipeline to build and deploy to a production-ready environment.
-    *   Continuously check the latest GitHub Actions run on the `main` branch until it's "completed" and "success".
-    *   Identify the job responsible for production deployment.
-    *   Extract the production URL.
-    *   Present the production URL to the user.
-9.  **Run Integration Tests (if applicable):**
-    *   (Optional) If a separate integration test suite exists for production, execute and monitor these tests.
-    *   Report test results to the user.

-    *   Ask me for final confirmation of the production deployment.

diff --git a/release_automation_prompt.md b/release_automation_prompt.md
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..52cbd10
--- /dev/null
+++ b/release_automation_prompt.md
@@ -0,0 +1,33 @@
+1.  **List all staged changes and unstaged changes** in detail, then ask me if I want to stage all outstanding changes.
+2.  **Generate a comprehensive commit message** (summarizing the staged changes) 
+3.  **Show the commit message** and ask for confirmation that I want to continue using this commit message
+4.  If I confirm that I want to continue, then **commit the staged changes** using the generated commit message, **push the branch**, and **create a Pul
l Request**. The PR title should be derived from the commit message's subject line, and the PR body should be the full commit message. Then, **monitor th
e deployment process** as described in step 5.
+5.  **Monitor deployment and provide preview link:**
+    *   Continuously check the latest GitHub Actions run on the current branch (`gh run list --branch <branch-name> --limit 1 --json status,url`).      
+    *   Once the run's `status` is "completed" and `conclusion` is "success", get the run ID.
+    *   Retrieve job details for that run (`gh run view <run_id> --json jobs`) to identify the `databaseId` of the "Deploy Preview (PR)" job.
+    *   Fetch the full logs for this job (`gh run view <run_id> --job <job_database_id> --log`).
+    *   Extract the preview URL from the logs. It will be a line similar to `"url": "https://<firebase-project-id>--pr-<pr-number>-<random-string>.web.a
pp"`, where `<firebase-project-id>` is the Firebase project ID for the staging environment.
+    *   Present the preview link to the user and ask them to confirm if it works as expected.
+6.  If I confirm that the preview link works as expected, then **merge the branch** so that the production is updated.
+7.  **Initiate Release Workflow (Post-Merge):**
+    *   **Determine new version:** Ask me if I want to bump the version as 'patch', 'minor', or 'major'.
+    *   **Update `package.json` & Commit (on `main`):** Update the `version` field in `package.json` based on my input and commit this change with a mes
sage like "Release vX.Y.Z" directly on the `main` branch.
+    *   **Create Git Tag (on `main`):** Create an annotated Git tag `vX.Y.Z` on the release commit on the `main` branch.
+    *   **Push to `main` & Tags:** Push the `main` branch (with the new version commit) and the new Git tag to the remote.
+8.  **Deploy Production Artifacts:**
+    *   Trigger the CI/CD pipeline to build and deploy to a production-ready environment.
+    *   Continuously check the latest GitHub Actions run on the `main` branch until it's "completed" and "success".
+    *   Identify the job responsible for production deployment.
+    *   get the production url using firebase CLI 
+    *   Present the production URL to the user.
+9.  **Run Integration Tests (if applicable):**
+    *   (Optional) If a separate integration test suite exists for production (e.g., indicated by a script like `test:integration` in `package.json`), e
xecute and monitor these tests.
+    *   Report test results to the user.
+10. **Final Confirmation & Handoff:**
+    *   Ask me for final confirmation of the production deployment.
+    *   Consider the task complete and await further instructions.
+11. **Prepare for Next Development Cycle:**
+    *   **Create and switch to a new development branch.** This ensures that further development work does not directly affect the main codebase (`main`
 branch) before being thoroughly tested in development and staging environments.
+    *   I will ask you if you have a specific name for this new branch, perhaps related to the next feature or bug fix you plan to work on. If not, I wi
ll suggest a default name like `feature/next-development`.
+    *   Once the branch is created and switched to, I will perform an empty commit on it with the message "FIRST EMPTY COMMIT for branch <branch name>" 
to establish its history.
\ No newline at end of file

+import { useState, useEffect } from 'react';

+// Fonction pour capitaliser la première lettre
+const capitalize = (s) => s.charAt(0).toUpperCase() + s.slice(1);
+
 export default function Header({ user, onLogout, isAudioEnabled, onEnableAudio, titleSuffix }) {
   const prNumber = import.meta.env.VITE_GITHUB_PR_NUMBER;
   const currentMode = import.meta.env.MODE;

+  const [dateTimeString, setDateTimeString] = useState('');
+
+  // --- MOCK DATA POUR LA MÉTÉO ---
+  // Remplacez ces données par un appel à une API météo (ex: OpenWeatherMap, WeatherAPI)
+  const weatherData = {
+    description: 'Ensoleillé',
+    temperature: 22,
+  };
+  // ---------------------------------
+
+  useEffect(() => {
+    const formatDateTime = () => {
+      const now = new Date();
+      const datePart = new Intl.DateTimeFormat('fr-FR', {
+        weekday: 'long',
+        day: 'numeric',
+        month: 'long',
+        year: 'numeric',
+      }).format(now);
+
+      const timePart = new Intl.DateTimeFormat('fr-FR', {
+        hour: '2-digit',
+        minute: '2-digit',
+        hour12: false,
+      }).format(now).replace(':', 'h');
+      
+      return `${capitalize(datePart)} - ${timePart}`;
+    };
+
+    const intervalId = setInterval(() => {
+      const formattedString = `${formatDateTime()} - ${weatherData.description} - ${weatherData.temperature}°C`;
+      setDateTimeString(formattedString);
+    }, 1000);
+
+    // Nettoyage de l'intervalle quand le composant est démonté
+    return () => clearInterval(intervalId);
+
+  }, []); // L'effet ne s'exécute qu'une seule fois au montage
+

messages.content: System: Please continue.

Okay, I've reviewed the staged changes, which include new GitHub workflows, renamed prompt files, UI enhancements, and feature implementations across `App.jsx`, `Header.jsx`, `RequirementsCard.jsx`, and `StatusDashboard.jsx`, along with `.gitignore` and `gemini.md` updates.

Here's a draft commit message summarizing these changes, following Conventional Commits guidelines:

```
feat: Implement interactive features, CI/CD integration, and UI enhancements

This commit introduces several new features, improvements, and CI/CD integrations across the Mictlan Dashboard project.

- **Features & Enhancements:**
  - **StatusDashboard:** Implemented interactive alerts with clickable toggling and audible alarms. Alerts include "Personnel", "Temps d'Attente", and "Avis en Ligne" with distinct statuses.
  - **RequirementsCard:** Added save functionality for 'Cahier des Charges' content to Firestore, including loading and feedback states.
  - **Header:** Integrated a dynamic date, time, and mock weather display into the main header.
  - **App.jsx:** Implemented role-based conditional rendering for `FeaturesCard` and `RequirementsCard`, showing them only to 'admin' users.

- **CI/CD & Automation:**
  - **GitHub Actions Integration:** Added new GitHub workflow files (`gemini-dispatch.yml`, `gemini-invoke.yml`, `gemini-review.yml`, `gemini-scheduled-triage.yml`, `gemini-triage.yml`) and their corresponding command definitions. These enable automated PR reviews, issue triage, and general Gemini CLI invocation within the CI pipeline.
  - **Prompt Renaming:** Renamed `next_pr_script_prompt.md` to `release_automation_prompt.md` for better clarity on its role in the release process.

- **Chore & Configuration:**
  - **.gitignore:** Updated to include `.gemini/` and `gha-creds-*.json` for cleaner repository management.
  - **gemini.md:** Updated model configuration documentation to include new "Start settings" and set `distribute_requests` default to `true`.
```

Please confirm if you wish to use this commit message.

args.content:
feat: Implement interactive features, CI/CD integration, and UI enhancements

This commit introduces several new features, improvements, and CI/CD integrations across the Mictlan Dashboard project.

- **Features & Enhancements:**
  - **StatusDashboard:** Implemented interactive alerts with clickable toggling and audible alarms. Alerts include "Personnel", "Temps d'Attente", and "Avis en Ligne" with distinct statuses.
  - **RequirementsCard:** Added save functionality for 'Cahier des Charges' content to Firestore, including loading and feedback states.
  - **Header:** Integrated a dynamic date, time, and mock weather display into the main header.
  - **App.jsx:** Implemented role-based conditional rendering for `FeaturesCard` and `RequirementsCard`, showing them only to 'admin' users.

- **CI/CD & Automation:**
  - **GitHub Actions Integration:** Added new GitHub workflow files (`gemini-dispatch.yml`, `gemini-invoke.yml`, `gemini-review.yml`, `gemini-scheduled-triage.yml`, `gemini-triage.yml`) and their corresponding command definitions. These enable automated PR reviews, issue triage, and general Gemini CLI invocation within the CI pipeline.
  - **Prompt Renaming:** Renamed `next_pr_script_prompt.md` to `release_automation_prompt.md` for better clarity on its role in the release process.

- **Chore & Configuration:**
  - **.gitignore:** Updated to include `.gemini/` and `gha-creds-*.json` for cleaner repository management.
  - **gemini.md:** Updated model configuration documentation to include new "Start settings" and set `distribute_requests` default to `true`.

response.output:
1.  **List all staged changes and unstaged changes** in detail, then ask me if I want to stage all outstanding changes.
2.  **Generate a comprehensive commit message** (summarizing the staged changes) 
3.  **Show the commit message** and ask for confirmation that I want to continue using this commit message
4.  If I confirm that I want to continue, then **commit the staged changes** using the generated commit message, **push the branch**, and **create a Pull Request**. The PR title should be derived from the commit message's subject line, and the PR body should be the full commit message. Then, **monitor the deployment process** as described in step 5.
5.  **Monitor deployment and provide preview link:**
    *   Continuously check the latest GitHub Actions run on the current branch (`gh run list --branch <branch-name> --limit 1 --json status,url`).
    *   Once the run's `status` is "completed" and `conclusion` is "success", get the run ID.
    *   Retrieve job details for that run (`gh run view <run_id> --json jobs`) to identify the `databaseId` of the "Deploy Preview (PR)" job.
    *   Fetch the full logs for this job (`gh run view <run_id> --job <job_database_id> --log`).
    *   Extract the preview URL from the logs. It will be a line similar to `"url": "https://<firebase-project-id>--pr-<pr-number>-<random-string>.web.app"`, where `<firebase-project-id>` is the Firebase project ID for the staging environment.
    *   Present the preview link to the user and ask them to confirm if it works as expected.
6.  If I confirm that the preview link works as expected, then **merge the branch** so that the production is updated.
7.  **Initiate Release Workflow (Post-Merge):**
    *   **Determine new version:** Ask me if I want to bump the version as 'patch', 'minor', or 'major'.
    *   **Update `package.json` & Commit (on `main`):** Update the `version` field in `package.json` based on my input and commit this change with a message like "Release vX.Y.Z" directly on the `main` branch.
    *   **Create Git Tag (on `main`):** Create an annotated Git tag `vX.Y.Z` on the release commit on the `main` branch.
    *   **Push to `main` & Tags:** Push the `main` branch (with the new version commit) and the new Git tag to the remote.
8.  **Deploy Production Artifacts:**
    *   Trigger the CI/CD pipeline to build and deploy to a production-ready environment.
    *   Continuously check the latest GitHub Actions run on the `main` branch until it's "completed" and "success".
    *   Identify the job responsible for production deployment.
    *   get the production url using firebase CLI 
    *   Present the production URL to the user.
9.  **Run Integration Tests (if applicable):**
    *   (Optional) If a separate integration test suite exists for production (e.g., indicated by a script like `test:integration` in `package.json`), execute and monitor these tests.
    *   Report test results to the user.
10. **Final Confirmation & Handoff:**
    *   Ask me for final confirmation of the production deployment.
    *   Consider the task complete and await further instructions.
11. **Prepare for Next Development Cycle:**
    *   **Create and switch to a new development branch.** This ensures that further development work does not directly affect the main codebase (`main` branch) before being thoroughly tested in development and staging environments.
    *   I will ask you if you have a specific name for this new branch, perhaps related to the next feature or bug fix you plan to work on. If not, I will suggest a default name like `feature/next-development`.
    *   Once the branch is created and switched to, I will perform an empty commit on it with the message "FIRST EMPTY COMMIT for branch <branch name>" to establish its history.

toolCalls.description: Reads and returns the content of a specified file. If the file is large, the content will be truncated. The tool's response will clearly indicate if truncation has occurred and will provide details on how to read more of the file using the 'offset' and 'limit' parameters. Handles text, images (PNG, JPG, GIF, WEBP, SVG, BMP), audio files (MP3, WAV, AIFF, AAC, OGG, FLAC), and PDF files. For text files, it can read specific line ranges.

toolCalls.description:
Replaces text within a file. By default, replaces a single occurrence, but can replace multiple occurrences when `expected_replacements` is specified. This tool requires providing significant context around the change to ensure precise targeting. Always use the read_file tool to examine the file's current content before attempting a text replacement.
      
      The user has the ability to modify the `new_string` content. If modified, this will be stated in the response.
      
      Expectation for required parameters:
      1. `old_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace (including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.).

      3. `instruction` is the detailed instruction of what needs to be changed. It is important to Make it specific and detailed so developers or large language models can understand what needs to be changed and perform the changes on their own if necessary. 
      4. NEVER escape `old_string` or `new_string`, that would break the exact literal text requirement.
      **Important:** If ANY of the above are not satisfied, the tool will fail. CRITICAL for `old_string`: Must uniquely identify the single instance to change. Include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. If this string matches multiple locations, or does not match exactly, the tool will fail.
      5. Prefer to break down complex and long changes into multiple smaller atomic calls to this tool. Always check the content of the file after changes or not finding a string to match.
      **Multiple replacements:** Set `expected_replacements` to the number of occurrences you want to replace. The tool will replace ALL occurrences that match `old_string` exactly. Ensure the number of replacements matches your expectation.

args.content:
feat: Implement interactive features, CI/CD integration, and UI enhancements

This commit introduces several new features, improvements, and CI/CD integrations across the Mictlan Dashboard project.

- **Features & Enhancements:**
  - **StatusDashboard:** Implemented interactive alerts with clickable toggling and audible alarms. Alerts include "Personnel", "Temps d'Attente", and "Avis en Ligne" with distinct statuses.
  - **RequirementsCard:** Added save functionality for 'Cahier des Charges' content to Firestore, including loading and feedback states.
  - **Header:** Integrated a dynamic date, time, and mock weather display into the main header.
  - **App.jsx:** Implemented role-based conditional rendering for `FeaturesCard` and `RequirementsCard`, showing them only to 'admin' users.

- **CI/CD & Automation:**
  - **GitHub Actions Integration:** Added new GitHub workflow files (`gemini-dispatch.yml`, `gemini-invoke.yml`, `gemini-review.yml`, `gemini-scheduled-triage.yml`, `gemini-triage.yml`) and their corresponding command definitions. These enable automated PR reviews, issue triage, and general Gemini CLI invocation within the CI pipeline.
  - **Prompt Renaming:** Renamed `next_pr_script_prompt.md` to `release_automation_prompt.md` for better clarity on its role in the release process.

- **Chore & Configuration:**
  - **.gitignore:** Updated to include `.gemini/` and `gha-creds-*.json` for cleaner repository management.
  - **gemini.md:** Updated model configuration documentation to include new "Start settings" and set `distribute_requests` default to `true`.

toolCalls.description:
Replaces text within a file. By default, replaces a single occurrence, but can replace multiple occurrences when `expected_replacements` is specified. This tool requires providing significant context around the change to ensure precise targeting. Always use the read_file tool to examine the file's current content before attempting a text replacement.
      
      The user has the ability to modify the `new_string` content. If modified, this will be stated in the response.
      
      Expectation for required parameters:
      1. `old_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace (including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.).
      2. `new_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace `old_string` with (also including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.). Ensure the resulting code is correct and idiomatic and that `old_string` and `new_string` are different.
      3. `instruction` is the detailed instruction of what needs to be changed. It is important to Make it specific and detailed so developers or large language models can understand what needs to be changed and perform the changes on their own if necessary. 
      4. NEVER escape `old_string` or `new_string`, that would break the exact literal text requirement.
      **Important:** If ANY of the above are not satisfied, the tool will fail. CRITICAL for `old_string`: Must uniquely identify the single instance to change. Include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. If this string matches multiple locations, or does not match exactly, the tool will fail.
      5. Prefer to break down complex and long changes into multiple smaller atomic calls to this tool. Always check the content of the file after changes or not finding a string to match.
      **Multiple replacements:** Set `expected_replacements` to the number of occurrences you want to replace. The tool will replace ALL occurrences that match `old_string` exactly. Ensure the number of replacements matches your expectation.

toolCalls.description:
This tool executes a given shell command as `powershell.exe -NoProfile -Command <command>`. Command can start background processes using PowerShell constructs such as `Start-Process -NoNewWindow` or `Start-Job`.

      The following information is returned:

      Command: Executed command.
      Directory: Directory where command was executed, or `(root)`.
      Stdout: Output on stdout stream. Can be `(empty)` or partial on error and for any unwaited background processes.
      Stderr: Output on stderr stream. Can be `(empty)` or partial on error and for any unwaited background processes.
      Error: Error or `(none)` if no error was reported for the subprocess.
      Exit Code: Exit code or `(none)` if terminated by signal.
      Signal: Signal number or `(none)` if no signal was received.
      Background PIDs: List of background processes started or `(none)`.
      Process Group PGID: Process group started or `(none)`

messages.content: Success: The "CI/CD — Dashboard Mictlan (consolidated)" run, ID `21419356094`, completed successfully, indicating a likely production deployment. Next, I'll pinpoint the production deployment job within this run using its `databaseId`, then extract the production URL via the Firebase CLI.

thoughts.subject: Examining Relevant Success
thoughts.description: I've just reviewed a successful run from the CI/CD pipeline, focusing on the "Dashboard Mictlan" project. The run completed successfully, and the conclusion was "success." I'm pulling the details to analyze the specific steps that led to this positive outcome, looking for patterns.

thoughts.subject: Identifying Deployment Steps

My aim is to isolate the job that leverages `firebase CLI` for the deployment. Once found, I'll extract the production URL.

I will now get the full logs for this job using `gh run view 21419356094 --job 61675216891 --log` to extract the production URL. If not found, I'll use `firebase hosting:list`.

response.output:
1.  **List all staged changes and unstaged changes** in detail, then ask me if I want to stage all outstanding changes.
2.  **Generate a comprehensive commit message** (summarizing the staged changes) 
3.  **Show the commit message** and ask for confirmation that I want to continue using this commit message
4.  If I confirm that I want to continue, then **commit the staged changes** using the generated commit message. To handle multi-line messages and special characters robustly, the commit message will first be written to a temporary file, and then `git commit -F <temp_file>` will be executed, followed by the deletion of the temporary file. After committing, **push the branch**, and **create a Pull Request**. The PR title should be derived from the commit message's subject line, and the PR body should be the full commit message. Then, **monitor the deployment process** as described in step 5.
5.  **Monitor deployment and provide preview link:**
    *   Continuously check the latest GitHub Actions run on the current branch (`gh run list --branch <branch-name> --limit 1 --json status,url`).
    *   Once the run's `status` is "completed" and `conclusion` is "success", get the run ID.
    *   Retrieve job details for that run (`gh run view <run_id> --json jobs`) to identify the `databaseId` of the "Deploy Preview (PR)" job.
    *   Fetch the full logs for this job (`gh run view <run_id> --job <job_database_id> --log`).
    *   Extract the preview URL from the logs. It will be a line similar to `"url": "https://<firebase-project-id>--pr-<pr-number>-<random-string>.web.app"`, where `<firebase-project-id>` is the Firebase project ID for the staging environment.
    *   Present the preview link to the user and ask them to confirm if it works as expected.
6.  If I confirm that the preview link works as expected, then **merge the branch** so that the production is updated.
7.  **Initiate Release Workflow (Post-Merge):**
    *   **Determine new version:** Ask me if I want to bump the version as 'patch', 'minor', or 'major'.
    *   **Update `package.json` & Commit (on `main`):** Update the `version` field in `package.json` based on my input and commit this change with a message like "Release vX.Y.Z" directly on the `main` branch.
    *   **Create Git Tag (on `main`):** Create an annotated Git tag `vX.Y.Z` on the release commit on the `main` branch.
    *   **Push to `main` & Tags:** Push the `main` branch (with the new version commit) and the new Git tag to the remote.
8.  **Deploy Production Artifacts:**
    *   Trigger the CI/CD pipeline to build and deploy to a production-ready environment.
    *   Continuously check the latest GitHub Actions run on the `main` branch until it's "completed" and "success".
    *   Identify the job responsible for production deployment.
    *   get the production url using firebase CLI, don't use the logs as you did for the PR preview URL
    *   Present the production URL to the user.
9.  **Run Integration Tests (if applicable):**
    *   (Optional) If a separate integration test suite exists for production (e.g., indicated by a script like `test:integration` in `package.json`), execute and monitor these tests.
    *   Report test results to the user.
10. **Final Confirmation & Handoff:**
    *   Ask me for final confirmation of the production deployment.
    *   Consider the task complete and await further instructions.
11. **Prepare for Next Development Cycle:**
    *   **Create and switch to a new development branch.** This ensures that further development work does not directly affect the main codebase (`main` branch) before being thoroughly tested in development and staging environments.
    *   I will ask you if you have a specific name for this new branch, perhaps related to the next feature or bug fix you plan to work on. If not, I will suggest a default name like `feature/next-development`.
    *   Once the branch is created and switched to, I will perform an empty commit on it with the message "FIRST EMPTY COMMIT for branch <branch name>" to establish its history.
12. **Now reflect on the issues you got running this prompt to modify it for next run

args.new_string:
1.  **List all staged changes and unstaged changes** in detail, then ask me if I want to stage all outstanding changes.
2.  **Generate a comprehensive commit message** (summarizing the staged changes) 
3.  **Show the commit message** and ask for confirmation that I want to continue using this commit message
4.  If I confirm that I want to continue, then:
    *   **Write the generated commit message to a temporary file** (e.g., `commit_message.txt` in the project root).
    *   **Commit the staged changes** using `git commit -F commit_message.txt`.
    *   **Delete the temporary file** `commit_message.txt`.
    *   **Push the branch**.
    *   **Write the full commit message (including subject and body) to a temporary file** (e.g., `pr_body.txt` in the project root).
    *   **Create a Pull Request**. The PR title should be derived from the commit message's subject line, and the PR body should be read from `pr_body.txt` (e.g., `gh pr create --base main --head <branch-name> --title "<PR_TITLE>" --body-file pr_body.txt`).
    *   **Delete the temporary file** `pr_body.txt`.
    *   Then, **monitor the deployment process** as described in step 5.
5.  **Monitor deployment and provide preview link:**
    *   Identify the PR number (if applicable).
    *   Continuously check the latest GitHub Actions runs for the relevant branch/PR using `gh run list --branch <branch-name> --json status,conclusion,url,name,databaseId`. If a specific PR is being monitored, use `gh run list --pr <pr-number> ...`. You might need to filter by workflow name (e.g., "Deploy Preview (PR)" or "CI/CD") to find the correct one if multiple workflows run.
    *   Once the run's `status` is "completed" and `conclusion` is "success", get the run ID.
    *   Retrieve job details for that run (`gh run view <run_id> --json jobs`) to identify the `databaseId` of the "Deploy Preview (PR)" job.
    *   Fetch the full logs for this job (`gh run view <run_id> --job <job_database_id> --log`).
    *   Extract the preview URL from the logs. It will be a line similar to `"url": "https://<firebase-project-id>--pr-<pr-number>-<random-string>.web.app"`, where `<firebase-project-id>` is the Firebase project ID for the staging environment.
    *   Present the preview link to the user and ask them to confirm if it works as expected.
6.  If I confirm that the preview link works as expected, then:
    *   **Check for any local changes** to any files, specifically `release_automation_prompt.md` if it has been modified during the run.
    *   If there are local changes, **stash them** using `git stash push -m "Stash changes before merge"`.
    *   **Merge the branch** so that the production is updated (e.g., `gh pr merge <pr-number> --merge --delete-branch`).
    *   **If a stash was created, reapply it** using `git stash pop`.
7.  **Initiate Release Workflow (Post-Merge):**
    *   **Determine new version:** Ask me if I want to bump the version as 'patch', 'minor', or 'major'.
    *   **Update `package.json` & Commit (on `main`):**
        *   Update the `version` field in `package.json` based on my input.
        *   **Stage `package.json`** (`git add package.json`).
        *   **Write the commit message** (e.g., "Release vX.Y.Z") to a temporary file (`commit_message.txt`).
        *   **Commit this change** using `git commit -F commit_message.txt` directly on the `main` branch.
        *   **Delete the temporary file** `commit_message.txt`.
    *   **Create Git Tag (on `main`):** Create an annotated Git tag `vX.Y.Z` on the release commit on the `main` branch.
    *   **Push to `main` & Tags:** Push the `main` branch (with the new version commit) and the new Git tag to the remote.
8.  **Deploy Production Artifacts:**
    *   Trigger the CI/CD pipeline to build and deploy to a production-ready environment.
    *   Continuously check the latest GitHub Actions run on the `main` branch until it's "completed" and "success".
    *   Identify the job responsible for production deployment.
    *   get the production url using firebase CLI, don't use the logs as you did for the PR preview URL
    *   Present the production URL to the user.
9.  **Run Integration Tests (if applicable):**
    *   (Optional) If a separate integration test suite exists for production (e.g., indicated by a script like `test:integration` in `package.json`), execute and monitor these tests.
    *   Report test results to the user.
10. **Final Confirmation & Handoff:**
    *   Ask me for final confirmation of the production deployment.
    *   Consider the task complete and await further instructions.
11. **Prepare for Next Development Cycle:**
    *   **Create and switch to a new development branch.** This ensures that further development work does not directly affect the main codebase (`main` branch) before being thoroughly tested in development and staging environments.
    *   I will ask you if you have a specific name for this new branch, perhaps related to the next feature or bug fix you plan to work on. If not, I will suggest a default name like `feature/next-development`.
    *   Once the branch is created and switched to, I will perform an empty commit on it with the message "FIRST EMPTY COMMIT for branch <branch name>" to establish its history.
args.instruction:
Updating `release_automation_prompt.md` to address issues encountered during previous runs. Modifications include:
-   **Step 4 (Commit & PR Creation):** Enhanced to use temporary files for commit messages and PR bodies with `git commit -F` and `gh pr create --body-file` respectively, ensuring robustness against shell escaping issues. Includes steps for creating and deleting these temporary files in the project root.
-   **Step 6 (Merge Branch):** Added steps to `git stash` local changes (specifically for the prompt file itself, if modified) before merging and `git stash pop` afterwards, preventing merge conflicts due to local modifications.
-   **Step 7 (Version Bump Commit):** Explicitly added `git add package.json` before committing the version bump to prevent "no changes added to commit" errors. Also integrated the temporary file approach for the commit message.
-   **Step 5 (Monitor Deployment):** Refined instructions for finding the correct GitHub Actions workflow run, suggesting filtering by PR number and workflow name for more robust identification of the deploy preview.

toolCalls.description:
Replaces text within a file. By default, replaces a single occurrence, but can replace multiple occurrences when `expected_replacements` is specified. This tool requires providing significant context around the change to ensure precise targeting. Always use the read_file tool to examine the file's current content before attempting a text replacement.
      
      The user has the ability to modify the `new_string` content. If modified, this will be stated in the response.
      
      Expectation for required parameters:
      1. `old_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace (including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.).
      2. `new_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace `old_string` with (also including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.). Ensure the resulting code is correct and idiomatic and that `old_string` and `new_string` are different.
      3. `instruction` is the detailed instruction of what needs to be changed. It is important to Make it specific and detailed so developers or large language models can understand what needs to be changed and perform the changes on their own if necessary. 
      4. NEVER escape `old_string` or `new_string`, that would break the exact literal text requirement.
      **Important:** If ANY of the above are not satisfied, the tool will fail. CRITICAL for `old_string`: Must uniquely identify the single instance to change. Include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. If this string matches multiple locations, or does not match exactly, the tool will fail.
      5. Prefer to break down complex and long changes into multiple smaller atomic calls to this tool. Always check the content of the file after changes or not finding a string to match.
      **Multiple replacements:** Set `expected_replacements` to the number of occurrences you want to replace. The tool will replace ALL occurrences that match `old_string` exactly. Ensure the number of replacements matches your expectation.

1.  **List all staged changes and unstaged changes** in detail, then ask me if I want to stage all outstanding changes.
2.  **Generate a comprehensive commit message** (summarizing the staged changes) 
3.  **Show the commit message** and ask for confirmation that I want to continue using this commit message
4.  If I confirm that I want to continue, then:
    *   **Write the generated commit message to a temporary file** (e.g., `commit_message.txt` in the project root).
    *   **Commit the staged changes** using `git commit -F commit_message.txt`.
    *   **Delete the temporary file** `commit_message.txt`.
    *   **Push the branch**.
    *   **Write the full commit message (including subject and body) to a temporary file** (e.g., `pr_body.txt` in the project root).
    *   **Create a Pull Request**. The PR title should be derived from the commit message's subject line, and the PR body should be read from `pr_body.txt` (e.g., `gh pr create --base main --head <branch-name> --title "<PR_TITLE>" --body-file pr_body.txt`).
    *   **Delete the temporary file** `pr_body.txt`.
    *   Then, **monitor the deployment process** as described in step 5.
5.  **Monitor deployment and provide preview link:**
    *   Identify the PR number (if applicable).
    *   Continuously check the latest GitHub Actions runs for the relevant branch/PR using `gh run list --branch <branch-name> --json status,conclusion,url,name,databaseId`. If a specific PR is being monitored, use `gh run list --pr <pr-number> ...`. You might need to filter by workflow name (e.g., "Deploy Preview (PR)" or "CI/CD") to find the correct one if multiple workflows run.
    *   Once the run's `status` is "completed" and "success", get the run ID.
    *   Retrieve job details for that run (`gh run view <run_id> --json jobs`) to identify the `databaseId` of the "Deploy Preview (PR)" job.
    *   Fetch the full logs for this job (`gh run view <run_id> --job <job_database_id> --log`).
    *   Extract the preview URL from the logs. It will be a line similar to `"url": "https://<firebase-project-id>--pr-<pr-number>-<random-string>.web.app"`, where `<firebase-project-id>` is the Firebase project ID for the staging environment.
    *   Present the preview link to the user and ask them to confirm if it works as expected.
6.  If I confirm that the preview link works as expected, then:
    *   **Check for any local changes** to any files, specifically `release_automation_prompt.md` if it has been modified during the run.
    *   If there are local changes, **stash them** using `git stash push -m "Stash changes before merge"`.
    *   **Merge the branch** so that the production is updated (e.g., `gh pr merge <pr-number> --merge --delete-branch`).
    *   **If a stash was created, reapply it** using `git stash pop`.
7.  **Initiate Release Workflow (Post-Merge):**
    *   **Determine new version:** Ask me if I want to bump the version as 'patch', 'minor', or 'major'.
    *   **Update `package.json` & Commit (on `main`):**
        *   Update the `version` field in `package.json` based on my input.
        *   **Stage `package.json`** (`git add package.json`).
        *   **Write the commit message** (e.g., "Release vX.Y.Z") to a temporary file (`commit_message.txt`).
        *   **Commit this change** using `git commit -F commit_message.txt` directly on the `main` branch.
        *   **Delete the temporary file** `commit_message.txt`.
    *   **Create Git Tag (on `main`):** Create an annotated Git tag `vX.Y.Z` on the release commit on the `main` branch.
    *   **Push to `main` & Tags:** Push the `main` branch (with the new version commit) and the new Git tag to the remote.
8.  **Deploy Production Artifacts:**
    *   Trigger the CI/CD pipeline to build and deploy to a production-ready environment.
    *   Continuously check the latest GitHub Actions run on the `main` branch until it's "completed" and "success".
    *   Identify the job responsible for production deployment.
    *   get the production url using firebase CLI, don't use the logs as you did for the PR preview URL
    *   Present the production URL to the user.
9.  **Run Integration Tests (if applicable):**
    *   (Optional) If a separate integration test suite exists for production (e.g., indicated by a script like `test:integration` in `package.json`), execute and monitor these tests.
    *   Report test results to the user.
10. **Final Confirmation & Handoff:**
    *   Ask me for final confirmation of the production deployment.
    *   Consider the task complete and await further instructions.
11. **Prepare for Next Development Cycle:**
    *   **Create and switch to a new development branch.** This ensures that further development work does not directly affect the main codebase (`main` branch) before being thoroughly tested in development and staging environments.
    *   I will ask you if you have a specific name for this new branch, perhaps related to the next feature or bug fix you plan to work on. If not, I will suggest a default name like `feature/next-development`.
    *   Once the branch is created and switched to, I will perform an empty commit on it with the message "FIRST EMPTY COMMIT for branch <branch name>" to establish its history.
args.instruction:
Updating `release_automation_prompt.md` to incorporate all lessons learned from the previous execution. This includes:
-   **Step 4 (Commit & PR Creation):** Detailed instructions for using temporary files for commit messages and PR bodies, ensuring shell-agnostic and robust handling of multiline strings and special characters.
-   **Step 6 (Merge Branch):** Added pre-merge checks for local changes to `release_automation_prompt.md` (or other files) and a stash/pop mechanism to prevent merge failures.
-   **Step 7 (Version Bump Commit):** Explicitly included `git add package.json` before committing the version update, addressing the "no changes added to commit" error. Also integrated the temporary file approach for this commit.
-   **Step 5 (Monitor Deployment):** Refined instructions for identifying the correct GitHub Actions run for deploy previews, emphasizing filtering by PR number and workflow name for accuracy.
These modifications aim to make the automation prompt more resilient, reliable, and clear for future executions.

resultDisplay.originalContent:
1.  **List all staged changes and unstaged changes** in detail, then ask me if I want to stage all outstanding changes.
2.  **Generate a comprehensive commit message** (summarizing the staged changes) 
3.  **Show the commit message** and ask for confirmation that I want to continue using this commit message
4.  If I confirm that I want to continue, then **commit the staged changes** using the generated commit message. To handle multi-line messages and special characters robustly, the commit message will first be written to a temporary file, and then `git commit -F <temp_file>` will be executed, followed by the deletion of the temporary file. After committing, **push the branch**, and **create a Pull Request**. The PR title should be derived from the commit message's subject line, and the PR body should be the full commit message. Then, **monitor the deployment process** as described in step 5.
5.  **Monitor deployment and provide preview link:**
    *   Continuously check the latest GitHub Actions run on the current branch (`gh run list --branch <branch-name> --limit 1 --json status,url`).
    *   Once the run's `status` is "completed" and `conclusion` is "success", get the run ID.
    *   Retrieve job details for that run (`gh run view <run_id> --json jobs`) to identify the `databaseId` of the "Deploy Preview (PR)" job.
    *   Fetch the full logs for this job (`gh run view <run_id> --job <job_database_id> --log`).
    *   Extract the preview URL from the logs. It will be a line similar to `"url": "https://<firebase-project-id>--pr-<pr-number>-<random-string>.web.app"`, where `<firebase-project-id>` is the Firebase project ID for the staging environment.
    *   Present the preview link to the user and ask them to confirm if it works as expected.
6.  If I confirm that the preview link works as expected, then **merge the branch** so that the production is updated.
7.  **Initiate Release Workflow (Post-Merge):**
    *   **Determine new version:** Ask me if I want to bump the version as 'patch', 'minor', or 'major'.
    *   **Update `package.json` & Commit (on `main`):** Update the `version` field in `package.json` based on my input and commit this change with a message like "Release vX.Y.Z" directly on the `main` branch.
    *   **Create Git Tag (on `main`):** Create an annotated Git tag `vX.Y.Z` on the release commit on the `main` branch.
    *   **Push to `main` & Tags:** Push the `main` branch (with the new version commit) and the new Git tag to the remote.
8.  **Deploy Production Artifacts:**
    *   Trigger the CI/CD pipeline to build and deploy to a production-ready environment.
    *   Continuously check the latest GitHub Actions run on the `main` branch until it's "completed" and "success".
    *   Identify the job responsible for production deployment.
    *   get the production url using firebase CLI, don't use the logs as you did for the PR preview URL
    *   Present the production URL to the user.
9.  **Run Integration Tests (if applicable):**
    *   (Optional) If a separate integration test suite exists for production (e.g., indicated by a script like `test:integration` in `package.json`), execute and monitor these tests.
    *   Report test results to the user.
10. **Final Confirmation & Handoff:**
    *   Ask me for final confirmation of the production deployment.
    *   Consider the task complete and await further instructions.
11. **Prepare for Next Development Cycle:**
    *   **Create and switch to a new development branch.** This ensures that further development work does not directly affect the main codebase (`main` branch) before being thoroughly tested in development and staging environments.
    *   I will ask you if you have a specific name for this new branch, perhaps related to the next feature or bug fix you plan to work on. If not, I will suggest a default name like `feature/next-development`.
    *   Once the branch is created and switched to, I will perform an empty commit on it with the message "FIRST EMPTY COMMIT for branch <branch name>" to establish its history.
12. **Now reflect on the issues** you got running this prompt to modify it for next run

toolCalls.description:
Replaces text within a file. By default, replaces a single occurrence, but can replace multiple occurrences when `expected_replacements` is specified. This tool requires providing significant context around the change to ensure precise targeting. Always use the read_file tool to examine the file's current content before attempting a text replacement.
      
      The user has the ability to modify the `new_string` content. If modified, this will be stated in the response.
      
      Expectation for required parameters:
      1. `old_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace (including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.).
      2. `new_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace `old_string` with (also including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.). Ensure the resulting code is correct and idiomatic and that `old_string` and `new_string` are different.
      3. `instruction` is the detailed instruction of what needs to be changed. It is important to Make it specific and detailed so developers or large language models can understand what needs to be changed and perform the changes on their own if necessary. 
      4. NEVER escape `old_string` or `new_string`, that would break the exact literal text requirement.
      **Important:** If ANY of the above are not satisfied, the tool will fail. CRITICAL for `old_string`: Must uniquely identify the single instance to change. Include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. If this string matches multiple locations, or does not match exactly, the tool will fail.
      5. Prefer to break down complex and long changes into multiple smaller atomic calls to this tool. Always check the content of the file after changes or not finding a string to match.
      **Multiple replacements:** Set `expected_replacements` to the number of occurrences you want to replace. The tool will replace ALL occurrences that match `old_string` exactly. Ensure the number of replacements matches your expectation.
